text: The surgeon uses a laparoscopic approach to run the small bowel, marking the pertinent points with silk sutures. The bowel is measured to create a 120-centimeter common channel and a 250-centimeter alimentary limb, with sutures placed at these specific points. The bowel is positioned to reach the future duodenal stump for anastomosis.

The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon identifies the pylorus region, placing a stitch for future reference. A sleeve gastrectomy is performed using a 40 French bougie, with the initial stapling done using a black cartridge, followed by green cartridges for subsequent firings.

The surgeon performs duodenal dissection by freeing and mobilizing the peritoneum superior and inferior to the duodenal bulb, creating a tunnel around the duodenum for stapler placement. The robotic platform is used for division without haptic feedback.

The surgeon uses the anvil part on the inferior edge to facilitate a hand-sewn anastomosis between the duodenum and ileum. A posterior row of Duovicro sutures is placed to reduce tension and align the duodenum and ileum. A hook cautery is used to create the duodenotomy and iliotomy, with a vessel sealer making the openings for the anastomosis. The surgeon creates a generous opening, unlike in gastric bypass, and runs Tool Vicro sutures from the midline, tying them together and using canal stitches around the corners.
The surgeon runs the small bowel distally to reach the 120-centimeter point, where the ileoileostomy is created. The common enterotomy is then closed using a running V-Loc suture.

The surgeon uses a permanent suture to perform a running closure of the mesenteric defect. The loop of ileum, previously brought up, is transected to create the Roux-en-Y configuration characteristic of the duodenal switch procedure.
The surgeon tests the hand-sewn anastomosis between the duodenum and ileum by insufflating air into the anastomosis while applying fluid to check for leaks.
